summary: In this 2007 lecture, Eric S. Lander reflects on the legacy of the Human Genome Project and outlines the future of genomic research. He emphasizes that biology has fundamentally become an information science, advocating for large-scale, goal-directed projects that make data freely and immediately available. He also discusses the power of comparative genomics, chromatin state maps, and unbiased genome-wide association studies to uncover the hidden circuitry of human disease.
themes: Open science and free data sharing
Human genetic variation and common diseases
Non-coding functional elements in the genome

principles.statement: Biology is fundamentally an information science.
principles.rationale: The greatest change in biology is the recognition that beyond studying organisms and molecules, biology is about comprehensive, genome-scale information that transforms the types of questions scientists can address.
principles.source_quote: ...at its very core, it is about information, and that there is genomic information.

principles.statement: Genomic information must be completely, freely, and immediately available.
principles.rationale: It is absurd to limit data access only to those who produce it. Enlisting the ideas and creativity of the entire global scientific community is crucial for maximizing the data's utility.
principles.source_quote: ...it was absolutely crucial that that information be completely and freely and immediately available to anybody because it was simply absurd that the people who were producing the projects were the only ones who could use it well.

principles.statement: Absolute completion shouldn't be the enemy of getting the vast majority of the information out.
principles.rationale: Waiting to close every single gap in a dataset prevents the scientific community from utilizing the 95-98% of the data that is already finished and highly useful.
principles.source_quote: Absolute completion shouldn't be the enemy of getting the vast majority of the information out.

principles.statement: Goal-directed science is highly effective for foundational infrastructure.
principles.rationale: Laying out clear goals, concrete plans, and timelines—even in the face of huge uncertainty—enables large-scale international collaboration to succeed.
principles.source_quote: Goal directed science had a bad name originally but the idea that if we thought clearly and had some things we had to get and we could define some goals, wouldn't be bad to lay out those goals and try to go for them...

principles.statement: Trust evolution's vote on what matters in the genome.
principles.rationale: If a genomic sequence is highly conserved across multiple mammalian species, it has an important biological function, even if a laboratory knockout experiment doesn't immediately reveal a phenotype.
principles.source_quote: If evolution tells you it's not willing to change that base, I go with evolution, it knows what it's doing.

frameworks.name: Genomic Information Project Playbook
frameworks.when_to_apply: When building foundational, large-scale biological datasets (e.g., genome sequencing, HapMap, mammalian radiation mapping) that require community-wide effort.
steps: Lay out clear, concrete goals and timelines, even in the face of uncertainty.
Establish international collaboration involving multiple countries and centers.
Build the necessary technological infrastructure and tools.
Make the resulting information completely, freely, and immediately available to the public.
Release the vast majority of the data (e.g., 95-98%) rather than waiting for absolute perfection.
frameworks.source_quote: We learned the importance of laying out concrete plans, timelines... And we learned the importance of collaboration. The importance of international collaboration.

mental_models.name: Biology as Information
mental_models.description: Viewing biological research not just as bench experiments on specific molecules, but as the extraction of insights from comprehensive, genome-scale datasets laid out for the entire world to analyze.
mental_models.example: Using a database of RNA expression signatures as a 'lingua franca' to connect disparate biological processes, allowing researchers to 'Google' a disease signature to find drugs that induce a similar or opposing cellular response.

mental_models.name: Cellular Development as Career Decisions
mental_models.description: A metaphor for cellular differentiation where a cell loses potential and makes commitments over time. Bivalent chromatin marks (both 'on' and 'off' modifications) indicate that a 'career decision' for that specific lineage still lies ahead.
mental_models.example: Observing that in embryonic stem cells, developmentally important genes have both red (inactive) and green (active) chromatin marks, keeping them poised for activation or repression depending on the lineage they eventually go down.

mental_models.name: Evolution as a Natural Experiment
mental_models.description: Treating the evolutionary divergence of species (e.g., the mammalian radiation) as a massive, pre-run experiment that highlights functional genomic elements through sequence conservation.
mental_models.example: Sequencing two dozen mammalian genomes (elephants, armadillos, bats, etc.) to refine the signal of conserved non-coding elements, revealing that the most conserved regions are often gene deserts enriched for developmentally important transcription factors.

heuristics: Absolute completion shouldn't be the enemy of getting the vast majority of the information out.
If evolution tells you it's not willing to change that base, I go with evolution, it knows what it's doing.
More is more: larger sample sizes in genetic studies yield exponentially more significant loci.
We are bad guessers: unbiased genome-wide approaches will outperform prior candidate gene lists.

signature_quotes.text: ...at its very core, it is about information, and that there is genomic information.
signature_quotes.context: Describing the single greatest change in biology over the last 20 years.

signature_quotes.text: Absolute completion shouldn't be the enemy of getting the vast majority of the information out.
signature_quotes.context: Discussing the release of the 'finished' human genome sequence despite having 300 remaining gaps.

signature_quotes.text: If evolution tells you it's not willing to change that base, I go with evolution, it knows what it's doing.
signature_quotes.context: Explaining why sequence conservation across species is a reliable indicator of functional importance.

signature_quotes.text: We're bad guessers. We're bad guessers about the common diseases as well.
signature_quotes.context: Reflecting on how genome-wide association studies revealed that the actual genes involved in common diseases were rarely the ones scientists had previously hypothesized.

signature_quotes.text: This is the world you guys are inheriting. This world where it is not just about the experiments on your own bench but the experiments of the entire world laid out before you to pick through and figure out how to extract the information from.
signature_quotes.context: Addressing the young scientists and post-docs in the audience about the future of biology.

anti_patterns.description: Waiting for 100% absolute completion before releasing scientific data.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: It delays scientific progress. Releasing the first 95-98% of the data immediately puts valuable information into the hands of thousands of scientists who can start making discoveries.

anti_patterns.description: Relying on 'prior candidate' gene lists to find the causes of complex diseases.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: Humans are 'bad guessers.' Unbiased, genome-wide association studies consistently show that the real risk factors are often unexpected and frequently located in non-coding regulatory regions.

anti_patterns.description: Restricting access to large-scale scientific data to only the groups that produced it.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: It is 'simply absurd' to think the producers are the only ones who can use it well; it fails to leverage the creativity and ideas of the global scientific community.
